coredata.dc:title: Assembly of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites with wide optical response and abnormal phase transition temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Youliang
Zhang, Yue
Fang, Changqing
Zhao, Tong
Zhao, Wei
Du, Yanli
Chen, Jing
coredata.dc:description: The rapid reversible metal-insulator transition (MIT) of vanadium dioxide (VO2) between the monoclinic and rutile phase brings about an abrupt change in optical properties. Until now, most researches focused on regulating near-infrared spectra and lowering phase transition temperature (Tc) while the optical response in the region of UV–vis and high Tc for VO2 are ignored. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced into mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and then CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites were synthesized in situ to expand the optical response and Tc range of VO2. Water bath time and annealing temperature have important influences on the morphologies and properties of as-prepared composites. The prolongation of hydrothermal time could promote the formation of VO2 nanoparticles. After annealing, the Tc for as-prepared CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites was in the range of 80.5–93.6 °C. The CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites exhibited broad and strong UV–vis absorption in the range of 200–1200 nm, and the sample prepared under the conditions of a water bath time of 3 h and annealing temperature of 550 °C had the maximum luminous transmittance (Tlum = 64.665 %) as well as the highest solar modulating ability (ΔTsol = 2.42 %). Therefore, the wide range of optical response and abnormal Tc besides typical phase transition behaviors for CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites have a potential application in the field of optical devices related to UV–vis region and high temperature environments.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid reversible metal-insulator transition (MIT) of vanadium dioxide (VO2) between the monoclinic and rutile phase brings about an abrupt change in optical properties. Until now, most researches focused on regulating near-infrared spectra and lowering phase transition temperature (Tc) while the optical response in the region of UV–vis and high Tc for VO2 are ignored. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced into mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and then CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites were synthesized in situ to expand the optical response and Tc range of VO2. Water bath time and annealing temperature have important influences on the morphologies and properties of as-prepared composites. The prolongation of hydrothermal time could promote the formation of VO2 nanoparticles. After annealing, the Tc for as-prepared CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites was in the range of 80.5–93.6 °C. The CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites exhibited broad and strong UV–vis absorption in the range of 200–1200 nm, and the sample prepared under the conditions of a water bath time of 3 h and annealing temperature of 550 °C had the maximum luminous transmittance (Tlum = 64.665 %) as well as the highest solar modulating ability (ΔTsol = 2.42 %). Therefore, the wide range of optical response and abnormal Tc besides typical phase transition behaviors for CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites have a potential application in the field of optical devices related to UV–vis region and high temperature environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanadium dioxide (VO2) as typical thermochromic materials will undergo a rapid reversible metal-insulator transition (MIT) between monoclinic phase VO2(M) and rutile phase VO2(R) at 68 °C. Abrupt changes in optical properties across MIT make VO2 as the promising materials for wide applications in smart windows [1,2], energy-saving coatings [3,4], and optoelectronic materials [3,5–7]. Therefore, this kind of materials have gained the attention of many researchers in recent years.

Some studies reported that VO2 with high Tc exhibited potential applications in aerospace and high-performance electronics [8,9]. Currently, functional devices that can withstand a high temperature (over 125 °C or even 150 °C) are needed in aerospace, military defense, high-performance electronics industries, especially resonators and filters needing to control the frequency, etc. [10]. Due to the unique and fast phase change characteristics, VO2 based materials can be applied in passive radiators, switches, modulators, storages, infrared camouflage, and resonators in aerospace as well as military fields [9]. Nevertheless, the intrinsic phase transition temperature (Tc, 68 °C) of VO2 is too low to support above functional devices in high temperature environments. Besides, the optical properties of VO2 itself are insufficient to exhibit the desired functions and effects.

Unfortunately, most previous reports mainly focused on decreasing the Tc of VO2 [11–13]. It was mostly achieved to increase Tc by doping with metal ions having small ionic radii such as Al3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+, yet this strategy is usually relatively inefficient [14]. It is necessary to develop more efficient route to increase Tc of VO2. In addition, the improvement of optical properties for VO2 based materials is also a key issue in current research. The MIT behaviors of VO2 enabled its optical response in the UV–vis, IR and even terahertz (THz) range [9]. However, most of the current researches about the optical response of VO2 are concentrated in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) light regions [15,16], and the response to UV light region has been neglected. In this regard, the introduction of photoluminescent materials, the construct of multilayer structures/films, the combination of silica nanocomposites and the formation of pore structures have been considered as the promising strategies to improve the optical properties of VO2 based materials [6,17–19]. However, the effective regulation of Tc and optical properties for VO2 still needs to be addressed in the future.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials have been applied in anti-counterfeiting [20], biology and fluorescence imaging [21,22] due to varieties of raw materials, facile synthesis, excellent photoluminescence, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [23–25]. Alternatively, CQDs can be combined with other materials to endow the composite with the optical properties of CQDs. Xie and co-workers [26] adopted a photoinduced method to prepare Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs catalysts, and found that the addition of CQDs gave the composites excellent upconversion fluorescence properties and light absorption ability resulting in the promotion of the light capture ability of the catalysts. Raikwar [27] fabricated CQDs@LaPO4:Eu3+ composites, which demonstrated that the introduction of CQDs increased the photoluminescence intensity of the composites by nearly twofold along with a high sensitivity. Cheng and co-workers [28] prepared CQDs/Ag NPs composites by UV-assisted in situ synthesis, which revealed that added CQDs broadened the light absorption range of Ag NPs.

According to above reports, we can adopt this strategy to introduce CQDs into VO2 based materials to endow the composite with photoluminescent properties. However, CQDs are highly prone to aggregate during the preparation process, which may have a negative effect on the performance of CQDs itself. To overcome this deficiency, CQDs can be loaded in solid carriers to improve the dispersibility [20]. Mesoporous silica has a high surface area and suitable pore size [29], which is the appropriate carrier of CQDs for improving its dispersion and optical performances. Fortunately, it is mentioned that the low refractive index of SiO2 can effectively improve the light transmission of VO2 coatings or films [30,31]. Thus, CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites were prepared in this work for imparting the photoluminescence property to VO2 based materials and expanding its optical response.

Herein, CQDs obtained by the hydrothermal method and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres prepared by the sol-gel method were self-assembled to form CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites, and then CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites were synthesized in situ. The morphology, structure, MIT behaviors and optical properties were investigated. For CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites, the Tc of VO2 based materials effectively increased and they exhibited special response in the UV region besides visible and NIR regions. This work may expand the application of VO2 in aerospace, high-performance electronics, bioimaging and thermochromic devices.

All reagents used in this experiment were of analytical grade and no purification was required. Glucose (C6H12O6) was provided by Shanghai Jiuding Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Cetane methyl ammonium bromide (C16H33(CH3)NBr) was supplied by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Urea was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Glycerol was purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylsilicate (TEOS) was provided by Bailingwei Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Oxalic acid (C2H2O4) was obtained from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

CQDs were prepared similarly to the previous report [32]. Typically, 0.6 g of C6H12O6 was dissolved in 90 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, above solution was transferred to a 100 mL reactor and kept in an oven at 180 °C for 24 h. Then, the system was naturally cooled to room temperature. Finally, the product was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was purified by a dialysis bag (3000 Da) in a dark environment to obtain CQDs.

Briefly, 1.2 g of CTAB was added to a three-necked flask containing 80 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred at a speed of 200 rpm in a water bath at 45 °C until the CTAB was dissolved. Next, 13.824 g of urea and 120 mL of deionized water were successively added to the three-neck flask. The mixture was heated with stirring at a speed of 80 rpm for 15 min in a water bath at 85 °C. Then, 32 mL of glycerol and 9.2 g of TEOS (28.8 mmol) were mixed in a beaker and subsequently added to a three-necked flask with a reflux condenser for reacting for 8 h. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The white precipitate after the centrifugation was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol, and then it was dried at 80 °C for 5 h. After calcined at 540 °C for 4 h, mesoporous SiO2 microspheres were obtained.

VO2 precursors were prepared according to the previously reported method [33]. Briefly, 4.6 g of V2O5 and 9.1 g of C2H2O4 were added to a three-necked flask containing 100 mL of deionized water. After the three-neck flask was equipped with a reflux condenser, the mixture was stirred at a speed of 80 rpm for 10 h in an oil bath at 120 °C to obtain VO2 precursor solution. The reaction formula during the formation of VO2 precursor is shown in Eq. (1).

Firstly, 0.075 g of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres were added to 5 mL of prepared CQDs solution. The mixture was stirred for 25 min to obtain CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composite solution. Secondly, 6 mL of VO2 precursor solution was added to 5 mL of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composite solution, and they were stirred in a water bath at 60 °C for 1–3 h. After the mixture was dried at 80 °C overnight, the product was annealed at a set temperature under Ar atmosphere to obtain CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites. The synthesis route is shown in Fig. 1 . As-prepared samples during this process were denoted as CSV-x-y, where x was water bath time and y was annealing temperature.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-3010) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JSM-3010) were utilized to observe the morphology of as-prepared products. For SEM testing, the unannealed samples were treated with gold (Pt) spray to improve the conductivity, and the annealed samples were simply pasted on a conductive adhesive. A zeta potential detector (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90) was used to measure the surface potential of CQDs and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres. FTIR spectra of as-prepared samples were obtained by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (8400S, Shimadzu) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The mixture of the sample and KBr was pressed to plates for FTIR testing. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD-7000, Shimadzu) was utilized to identify the phase state of products in the range of 2θ = 10°–70° at a scanning rate of 8°/min. A differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, DSC 200F3) was used to measure the Tc of products in the range of 0–120 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C/min and cooling rate of 10 °C/min. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (JASCO V-770) was utilized to measure the absorption spectra of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites, and deionized water was used as a control. A fluorescence spectrometer (PL, Quanta Master 8000, HORIBA) was used to measure the photoluminescence spectra of products. A UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 950) was used to measure the transmission spectra of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites in the range of 300–2500 nm at 20 °C and 120 °C, respectively.

The transmittance of samples was measured by making the powder samples to form thin films, and the specific processing was carried out by following steps. Typically, 0.1 g of CSV-x-y and 0.286 g of PVP were dispersed in 5 mL of ethanol to form a dispersion. Then, 0.5 mL of the dispersion was placed on a glass substrate (square with 4 cm sides and 1 mm thickness), and the CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composite film was made by using a spin coater (SPIN150i) under the conditions of 400 rpm for 20 s and 500 rpm for 30 s and through the drying in an oven at 60 °C for 5 min. The preparation illustration of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composite films is shown in Fig. 2 .

The optical properties of the samples were mainly described by transmittance and solar modulation ability. The following equations were used to calculate the luminous transmittance (Tlum, from 380 to 780 nm), solar transmittance (Tsol, from 300 to 2500 nm) and solar modulation ability (ΔTsol) of the samples [33,34]:

where T(λ) denotes the transmittance value at wavelength λ, φlum(λ) is the standard efficiency function for photopic vision, and φsol(λ) is the solar irradiance spectrum at an atmospheric mass of 1.5 corresponding to solar radiation when the sun is at 37°of the horizon. Tsol,20 °C and Tsol,120 °C correspond to Tsol at 20 °C and 120 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of CQDs and CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The size distribution of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and TEM image of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 3 . We analyzed the particle size of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres with the number of 102, and the size distribution of which is shown in Fig. 3(a). The average particle size of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres prepared by the sol-gel method was about 248.10 nm with the standard deviation of 4.66 nm (in Fig. 3(a)). Small amounts of CQDs were present on the surface of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres (in Fig. 3(b)). The surface potential of CQDs and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres displayed a negative potential of −46.153 mV and a positive potential of +24.007 mV, respectively. Due to the mutual attraction, mesoporous SiO2 microspheres with positive surface potential and CQDs with negative surface potential were spontaneously assembled together during the stirring. Therefore, this result proves that as-prepared CQDs and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres in this work can be assembled into CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites by electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water bath time on the morphology and structure of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows SEM images of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites obtained after different water bath time. These composites before the annealing still keep near-spherical shape, but the adhesion between these spheres is observed. After the water bath treatment, VO2 particles deposited on the surface of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites. Compared with sample CSV-1 and CSV-2, the surface layer of near-spherical composites for sample CSV-3 (in Fig. 4(c)) seems to be more uniform with the prolongation of the water bath time.

Fig. 5 displays TEM images of CQD/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites obtained after different water bath time. The VO2 precursor was converted to minute particles and deposited on the surface of the CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites, which is consistent with the SEM results. According to TEM images of CSV-1, CSV-2 and CSV-3, we observed that the amount of particles on the surface of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites increased continuously with the prolongation of water bath time. During the process of water bath stirring, VO2+ in the precursor will firmly contact with CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites, and the crystal nucleus sites will form on the surface of this composites. The number of crystal nucleus site for VO2 particles increases with the prolongation of the water bath time, and then the number of generated VO2 particles increases while the size decreases accordingly. In order to keep the minimum surface energy, the generated VO2 will cover the surface of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites uniformly [35], and then the surface coverage of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites by VO2 increases as the prolongation of the water bath time. This result reveals that the prolongation of the water bath time contributed to the formation of small VO2 particles and high surface coverage of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composite.

According to above analysis, VO2 particles on the surface of sample CSV-3 are more abundant and more uniform compared with sample CSV-1 and CSV-3 (in Figs. 4 and 5). Therefore, we chose the sample of CSV-3 to investigate the assembly effect of CQDs, mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and VO2 through energy spectrum scanning. Fig. 6 gives the energy spectrum of CSV-3 and the content of each element. It can be seen that C, Si, O and V element signals were showed for sample CSV-3. The atomic content of O and V was 49.21 % and 18.70 %, respectively, while the weight content of O and V was 32.86 % and 39.75 %, respectively. Although there may be other valence states for the V element in sample CSV-3 according to the atomic content of V, Si and O, these results still illustrate the successful assembly of CQDs, mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and VO2.

FTIR spectra of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 samples obtained after different water bath time are shown in Fig. 7(a). All samples have a broad and strong absorption band at about 3480 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH [36]. The absorption peaks at 2861, 1670, 1405 and 1310 cm−1 belonging to CQDs, were attributed to the stretching vibration of CH2 [37], the stretching vibration of CO [38], the CO symmetric stretching vibration of COO− [37], and the stretching vibration of CN [39], respectively. The adsorption band located around 806 cm−1 was ascribed to the symmetrical stretching vibration and bending vibration of SiOSi in mesoporous SiO2 microspheres [40]. The main characteristic absorption peaks of VO2 are in the range of 400–1200 cm−1 [41,42]. The absorption peak at 966 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching pattern of the VO bond in the distorted octahedral and quasi-pyramidal structures [43], and the absorption peak at 480 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of the octahedral angular deformation of VOV [42]. These results also demonstrate the successful preparation of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites. Additionally, the relative intensity for the absorption bands in 400–1200 cm−1 was enhanced due to the formation of more VO2 from precursors with the prolongation of water bath time.

Fig. 7(b) shows XRD patterns of sample CSV-1, CSV-2, and CSV-3. According to the standard PDF cards for pure VO2(P)(#73-0514) [44], it is observed that the diffraction peak positions of the three samples are basically same which belong to VO2(P), and there is no significant difference in the relative intensity. Additionally, we noticed some impure peaks in the XRD patterns appearing in the range of 2θ = 10–20°, which are attributed to V4O7. This result is possibly due to the reduction of VO2 particles triggered by CQDs on the surface of mesoporous SiO2 [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water bath time on the optical performances of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra were performed to further investigate the optical properties of the products. Fig. 8(a) and (b) show UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites, respectively. The sample CQDs exhibited two broad absorption peaks centered at 248 nm and 310 nm for UV–vis adsorption spectrum, which were attributed to π-π* (CC) and n-π* (CO/CN) transitions, respectively [46,47]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength for CQDs is 370 nm and 480 nm, respectively. Compared with CQDs, the relative intensity of UV–vis absorption and PL emission peaks for CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites was significantly enhanced, and the location for the optimal emission peak was the same as that of CQDs while the optimal excitation wavelength was slightly red-shifted. The relative intensity increase of PL emission for CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites is attributed to the improvement of the dispersion for CQDs. In addition, the change of the optimized excitation wavelength of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites is due to the interaction between CQDs and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres which changes the surface state of CQDs and causes the energy level of the excited state on the surface of CQDs to be shifted [48]. Moreover, the relative intensity of the optimal excitation and emission peaks for CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites remarkably increased, which may result from the reduction of surface defects and the low probability of non-radiative leap for CQDs after incorporated with mesoporous SiO2 microspheres [32].

The digital images of the CQDs solution and CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm) irradiation are shown in Fig. 8(c). The former emitted green fluorescence under UV light irradiation, and the latter emitted more brightly green fluorescence UV light irradiation according to the visualized result. The above results demonstrate that the PL properties of CQDs can be effectively regulated by combining with mesoporous SiO2 microspheres.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the products obtained after different water bath time are shown in Fig. 9(a). The CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites exhibited a characteristic absorption peak around 220 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond in CQDs [49,50]. In the range of 400–1200 nm, the absorption peaks at 600 and 780 nm were ascribed to the d-d transition of VO2+ in composite solution [51]. In addition, the relative intensity of the three absorption peaks for the CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites increased continuously with the prolongation of the water bath time. The relative intensity increase of the absorption peaks at 600 and 780 nm can be attributed to the higher concentration of VO2+ after longer water bath time [52]. Furthermore, the high concentration of VO2+ enhanced the interaction between CQDs and VO2+, leading to the increase of the light absorption intensity at 220 nm.

The fluorescence emission spectra of CSV-1, CSV-2, and CSV-3 are shown in Fig. 9(b). Notably, all samples exhibited three fluorescence emission peaks at 451, 468 and 514 nm when excited at 370 nm, but the relative intensity of the emission peaks was slightly different. The relative intensity at 451 and 468 nm was higher compared with the emission peak at 514 nm. The two emission peaks at 451 and 468 nm were assigned to intrinsic and defects emission [53], while the emission peak at 514 nm was belonged to extrinsic fluorescence, which may be due to the change of surface states of CQDs caused by the introduction of VO2 particles and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres [54]. In addition, it was found that the relative emission intensity of CSV-1 and CSV-2 was essentially the same and that of corresponding locations for CSV-3 decreased when the water bath time prolonged to 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of annealing temperature on the morphology and structure of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the SEM images of CSV-1, CSV-2 and CSV-3 after annealing at 450 °C and 550 °C, respectively. Compared with the samples before the annealing (in Fig. 3), the spherical shape of the products was still retained after the annealing, but a few plates were interspersed between these spheres, which are supposed to the successful assembly of VO2 particles on the surface of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres. The size of CQDs in the composites became smaller after the annealing [55], and the pore structure for the surface of mesoporous SiO2 microspheres can be observed obviously. The spherical products were more regular and occupy a larger proportion in the sample obtained at the same annealing temperature with the prolongation of water bath time, which may be due to the embedding of plate-like VO2 particles inside mesoporous SiO2 microspheres during the annealing process. Moreover, fewer impurities existed in the samples at the annealing temperature of 550 °C when the water bath time was fixed.

Based on above analysis, the sample of CSV-3 with strong UV–vis absorption showed a relatively regular morphology after the annealing, therefore, we conducted TEM analysis of the samples obtained after the annealing at 450 °C and 550 °C. Fig. 11 shows the morphologies of the sample CSV-3-450 and CSV-3-550. Obviously, VO2 particles were attached to the surface of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites before the annealing (in Fig. 4). After the annealing, most of the VO2 particles were embedded in the mesoporous SiO2 microspheres and changed to be lamellar products. Compared with the sample CSV-3-450, the lamellar VO2 in the sample CSV-3-550 is larger in size and the number is fewer which were consistent with the SEM analysis results (in Fig. 10(c) and (f)). The high-temperature annealing caused the VO2 nanoparticles to recrystallize and grow continuously, and the crystallinity of VO2 particles increased as the annealing temperature increased. Additionally, the combination of VO2 reduced the number of the particles and resulted in the increase of the size [56,57].

Fig. 12(a) shows the FTIR spectra of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites obtained after the annealing at 450 °C and 550 °C. All samples showed an absorption peak at 3480 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH. It was observed that the functional groups of the samples obtained at different annealing temperatures were significantly different. When the annealing temperature was 450 °C, obtained CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites showed a strong absorption peak at 1650 cm−1, which was slightly red-shifted compared with the absorption peak located at 1670 cm−1 before the annealing. In addition, CSV-2-450 and CSV-3-450 exhibited no obvious absorption peaks in the range of 400–1200 cm−1, but only a weak absorption peak at 1295 cm−1 which corresponded to a slight red-shift of the absorption peak located at 1310 cm−1 before annealing. The annealed samples at 550 °C revealed absorption peaks at 1670, 1402, 985, 810 and 480 cm−1, which have only slight changes in the location compared with that for the samples obtained before the annealing. Notably, the absorption peaks at 1295 and 1402 cm−1 were weakened and even disappeared after the annealing, which was attributable to the reduction of the functional group concentration of CQDs through the annealing process [55].

Fig. 12(b) shows the XRD patterns of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites obtained after the annealing at 450 °C and 550 °C. The XRD patterns of these samples are consistent with the pure VO2(M) standard PDF card (#43–1051) [58], which suggests that the annealing enables the transition of VO2(P) to VO2(M). Furthermore, the relative intensity of XRD diffraction peak for the products obtained at the annealing temperature of 550 °C is higher than that obtained at the annealing temperature of 450 °C, which illustrates the high annealing can improve the crystallinity of the products [59]. Therefore, the annealing temperature of 550 °C is preferable for the crystallization of VO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Abnormal phase transition temperature of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The DSC curves for the samples obtained at different annealing temperatures are shown in Fig. 13 . The Tc of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites obtained after the annealing treatment was in the range of 80.5–93.6 °C. Compared with pure VO2 (Tc of 68 °C) [60], the Tc for the six samples increased by 12–26 °C. The sample of CSV-3-450 has the lowest Tc of 80.5 °C. In addition, the Tc of the annealed samples at 550 °C was higher than those at 450 °C. The MIT of VO2 is considered to be similar to the martensitic transformation, where structural defects involving in grain boundaries, dislocations, oxygen vacancies and heteroatoms, will serve as nucleation sites for the phase transition of VO2. As the annealing temperature increases, the crystallinity of the products increases, and then the structural defects of the products and the corresponding nucleation sites will decrease. As a result, the Tc of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites increases accordingly, which is related with the nucleation sites [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performances of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites after annealing
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples obtained at different annealing temperatures. It is observed that the annealed sample has a characteristic absorption peak at 260 nm, which was caused by the π-π* leap of the sp2-hybridized CC bond in CQDs [61,62]. In addition, these annealed samples showed a very weak absorption peak at about 410 nm, which was ascribed to the surface group change of the CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites during the annealing [53].

Fig. 15 exhibits the PL spectra of the samples obtained at different annealing temperatures. Under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, all samples displayed PL emission peaks at 450 or 451 nm, 468 nm and 515 nm, the positions of which are almost the same as those obtained before the annealing (in Fig. 8(b)). Thus, the annealing treatment has few impacts on the emission peak location of the CQD/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites.

In this work, CQDs obtained by hydrothermal method and mesoporous SiO2 microspheres prepared by sol-gel method were firstly self-assembled to form CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 composites, then CQDs/mesoporous SiO2 microsphere/VO2 composites were synthesized in situ after adding VO2 precursor. During the synthesis in situ, the water bath time and annealing temperature have important effects on the morphology and structure of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites. In addition, the two factors also have effects on the MIT temperature and optical properties. The Tc of CQDs/mesoporous SiO2/VO2 composites can reach to 93.6 °C, and all samples exhibited a broad and strong UV–vis absorption in the range of 200–1200 nm. The composites prepared under the conditions of water bath time of 3 h and annealing temperature of 550 °C exhibited the maximum Tlum of 64.665 % as well as the highest ΔTsol of 2.42 %. Consequently, this work provides a suitable route to increase the Tc of VO2 and improve its optical properties.
